Hi @SpaceTeddy,

thank you for your contribution. We discussed the pull request and think that it is not the right way to integrate your work.
1. Every time you update/fix the code we have to fix the project code accordingly.
2. The current state of your implementation does not allow to encode data which is a must have for our decodings as long as this is possible (e.g. not for cut obviously).

We would like to integrate it in the following way: Our documentation will have a table for external extensions and plugins (or an explicit repo). There we can link your github project. What do you think?
Thank you!

Angles are considered to be dimensionless. In fact, anything that can't be defined in terms of a _physical_ unit is considered dimensionless. They may still have units, but units and dimensions are not the same. Check out [this answer](http://physics.stackexchange.com/a/193699/85874) for a better explanation of why.

```python
>>> '{:.2f}'.format(np.ones(3))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: non-empty format string passed to object.__format__
```
I am not sure it is a bug. It is certainly annoying but it is reproducing the way Python operates. Only valid format codes for the specific object can be used. For example:
```python
>>> '{:d}'.format(2)
'2'
>>> '{:d}'.format(2.0)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: Unknown format code 'd' for object of type 'float'
```
I do agree that the error message could be improved.

Hey,
Yes I totally agree I missed the sleep in transaction. I see I also forgot some asyncio calls in pubsub. I'd like to add some test to catch these, as it seems that code is never run on tests.

I don't think it would be a good idea to add a `loop` argument to the `Lock.__init__` method. It already gets a redis object which will have a loop argument. By adding it there is a chance that someone gives the system two different loops which is something you never want to do! But I do agree that the `get_connection().loop` part should be removed! I suggest to add the loop as an attribute to the `ConnectionPool` object. I could send in a pull request to change that if you want.

Lastly I think there is as you say one place where we have to guard for the loop being None and that is in the `PubsubWorkerThread` object.

Thanks accepting and reviewing my code! aredis is making my code a lot cleaner!

This is not really a leak; the code is doing what it is supposed to do. The `GVar` variables created in your last line are being discarded, but their variances are being retained (in case those variables were used to create other variables). To illustrate what is going on consider the following example:

    import gvar as gv
    import numpy as np

    def f():
        x = gv.gvar('1(1)')
        y = gv.gvar('2.0(5)')
        return [x + y, x - y, y * gv.cos(x / y), gv.log(x * y)]

    z = f()
    print(gv.evalcov(z))

Here the `GVar` variables `x` and `y` are discarded at the end of `f()`, but their variances are stored (globally) so that the covariances between the different components of `z` can be constructed when needed. The covariance matrix for the `z[i]` is printed by the last line and is:

    [[ 1.25        0.75       -0.20010171  1.125     ]
     [ 0.75        1.25       -0.75874937  0.875     ]
     [-0.20010171 -0.75874937  0.54193606 -0.33976362]
     [ 1.125       0.875      -0.33976362  1.0625    ]]

The variances (or covariances) for any new `GVar`(s) created using `gvar.gvar(...)` are automatically retained for this purpose. The `gvar` module would be a lot simpler if it wasn't required to capture the correlations between the different `z[i]`s.

This behavior doesn't seem to make sense for your example where you create 40 billion `GVar`s that you never use, but I assume that your real code actually does use these variables. If it is the case that the `GVar`s used in one iteration are of no relevance to any other iteration of the loop, then it is possible to avoid the memory build up by using a different version of the function `gvar.gvar` in each iteration:

    import gvar as gv
    import numpy as np

    for i in range(4000000):
        tmp_gvar = gv.gvar_factory()
        spectrum = np.arange(100)
        spectrum = tmp_gvar(spectrum, np.ones(100))

This hasn't been useful for the things I do, but it might be relevant for other work.

Error happens when restarting celery beat with redbeat because it looks for old schedule entries in Redis, which returns them as bytes. When comparing them to the current static schedule entries, redbeat thinks they need to be removed, because building a set difference between the Redis schedule keys (bytes) and the current static schedule keys (strings) always yields a non-empty set with the schedule keys as bytes. Redbeat thus tries to remove these schedule entries, which leas to the error cited above, because instantiating a `RedBeatSchedulerEntry` without passing a schedule or app instance is not handled correctly by the super-class `celery.beat.ScheduleEntry`.

On Python 3 the error should happen on every restart, on Python 2 every time you remove a static schedule entry and restart.

Basically, I am concerned whether writing UTF-8 is generally supported to network devices. I don't really have much data one way or another so am defaulting to doing what I am currently doing.

Yes, ASCII is a subset of UTF-8, but the question is what happens when you start sending non-ascii characters.

This will still give you a workaround for your problem, but you will have to specify the `write_encoding="utf-8"`.

Note, I am open to more data that network devices will generally support UTF-8 encoded bytes.

I agree with @ltpour that it is difficult to have general purpose instructions since XMonad is configured using a full programming language (and thus has innumerable ways in which it can be configured), but I can perhaps give it a shot:

`import System.Environment

import XMonad

-- Takes as input the name of an environment variable describing a color and a
-- xmonad configuration, and returns a new configuration with the normal border
-- color set to the color contained within the given environment
-- variable.
setNormalBorder :: String -> XConfig l -> IO (XConfig l)
setNormalBorder var conf = do
    color <- lookupEnv var
    return $ conf { normalBorderColor  = getColor color }
  where
    getColor (Just c)  = c
    getColor Nothing   = "#cccccc" -- Default to white if var is not found`

This assumes the wal colors have been exported as environment variables. Probably can be improved, but this should get the job done.

What's defined in Solaris is this:

     /usr/include/net/if.h:#define	lifr_mtu	lifr_lifru.lifru_mtu	/* mtu */

In Solaris it's lifr, not ifr, and that's what we use in the Solaris implementation of net_if_stats: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/_psutil_sunos.c#L1252 (this is the fix from #610)

For some reason the compilation tries to compile _psutil_posix.c with the wrong definition of ifr_mtu, but the line with this definition is wrapped with an ifdef:

    #if defined(__FreeBSD__) || defined(__OpenBSD__) || defined(__APPLE__) || defined(__NetBSD__)

so the compiler shouldn't really get there....

I suspect you're not using the latest version from this repository: your compiler hits the error on psutil/_psutil_posix.c:272 but this is a blank line in the current implementation: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/_psutil_posix.c#L272

We need something more sophisticated. Off the top of my head I would suggest the following: check whether data and metadata are consistent after the write operation (note: I write "consistent", not "equal to what we wrote") and if not, wait for a random delay (between 0.5 and 2 seconds maybe?) and re-try the write (maybe doubling the random range edges each time?). Impose a limit on the maximum number of writes attempted (maybe 3?) and if it isn’t consistent afterwards, raise an exception. Log when retries are needed.

Of course, we can *in addition* have a lock to prevent this situation between local users of the service.

The last commit fixes a huge memory leak where nassl was leaking an OpenSSL BIO on almost every connection.

In a simple script that runs 3 scan commands 20 times (https://gist.github.com/nabla-c0d3/fc633194f7a8509a7e68c931f1fbc471), memory usage at the end of the script went from 126856 kb to 50404 kb.
The memory was also relatively stable but was still increasing slightly on each of the 20 iterations. From my analysis, the biggest remaining leaks (which are much smaller than the big one I just fixed) are in OpenSSL 1.0.1e, mainly in `SSL_do_handshake()`; not much I can do about this. 

This blog post helped me a lot, especially the section about umdh : https://benbernardblog.com/tracking-down-a-freaky-python-memory-leak/#usingumdhtosearchforcbasedmemoryleaks

> 1) Why can't I specify my custom JSON encoder instead of the stock
> json.JSONEncoder?

No particular reason: it's just not been implemented (and the `text`
argument can be used if you want to control the encoding yourself).

> 2a) Why is this being converted to a string in the
> first place?

This was the easiest way of invoking jq as a library i.e. to give it a
string that should be parsed as JSON. It should also be possible to
construct jq structs directly, but using a string sufficed for me at
the time.

> 2b) Should I be dumping my nested dictionary/list JSON
> object to a string prior to using it in jq so I can use a custom JSON
> encoder?

If you want to use your own custom JSON encoder then yes, dumping it to
a string first would work. You'd then have to pass in the JSON text
using the `text` keyword argument. For instance:

    jq(program).transform({"a": 1})

should give the same result as:

    jq(program).transform(text='{"a": 1}')

> 2c) Is there any performance boost or penalty with the
> converting to string vs native Python objects?

At the moment, if you already have JSON text, then there's no point
parsing it since it'll get turned back into JSON text. If you're
starting with Python objects, it's up to you whether you pass those in
or whether to generate the JSON text yourself and use the `text`
argument. These bindings just use `json.dumps()`, so whether it's faster
to do it yourself or not depends on whether you can generate the JSON
text faster than `json.dumps()`.

> 3) Why is there
> literally no documentation on the `transform()` method? From the main
> README, I see there are 3 arguments possible with [0] always being
> the data object but two keyword args -- are there more? 

The README describes the various possible arguments you can pass in
with explanations and examples (none of which use [0] as the data
object), which I'd consider more than "literally no
documentation" (unless we're looking at different pages? I'm looking
at the README on GitHub [1]). There aren't any additional arguments
beyond those described.

[1] https://github.com/mwilliamson/jq.py

I admit the documentation is fairly short, but the interface is rather
small: most of the interesting functionality comes from the jq programs
you can write, for which it seems sensible to defer to their
documentation.

@shehzadi, I think you're a bit confused about how argparse works. The rule of thumb for argcomplete is that it won't generate an invalid completion (one that causes the argparse parser to throw an error). If we were to complete anything on a positional that we don't have any choices information for, the completions would not be valid (to our best knowledge, anyway). There is nothing to complete on a positional with no completion information. However, once you declare a subparser, we do have the ability to complete on the subparser name.

The main thing you need to do is just look at the form of arguments that you supply to your program, and look at how you can inform argcomplete about what to suggest at any point in the command line.

Ultimately, what you might be looking for is out-of-band help information for completions: in other words, in addition to suggesting completions to your command line, the tab completion driver would also print - not into the command line! - help information for the option currently under the cursor. I know Zsh has this capability, but unfortunately I'm not aware of any way for bash to support it (though perhaps I could try printing to stderr).

If you have any specific wording for a warning that you want me to insert into the doc (including examples), please feel free to paste it here.

One of the things that went into the release, as mentioned above, is to make sure datas of different timeframes deliver at the same time. Data feeds are based on *ticks* and knowing if a *resampled* bar is over or not depends on the next incoming tick.

To overcome this the code already included a *time based* check that delivers a *resampled* bar when the time has gone over the boundary of what would be the resampled bar (*IB* notifies of the time difference with the server to allow for a correct calculation of where the boundary really is)

The code wakes up to make checks and deliver a resampled bar if the time boundary has been exceeded even if no tick for the new period has yet been seen. The "waking up" is controlled by Python/Operating System and the timings are good but can create an out of sync problem.

As such and to avoid it, *slave* datas are given a 2nd chance with an extra check if they have nothing to deliver yet, but the *master* is already delivering.

**NOTE**: In the case of different timeframes, and unlike in the out-sync-case from above, the different length of the datas is not only expected: *IT MUST BE*, because they have different clock speeds and the indicators that operate on these *datas* operate on the exact amount of available data.

There is (for sure) a bug there, but your actual problem has to do with timing and I guess with expectations.

Your daily bars have the following time coded: `00:00:00`. Obviously, the daily bar can only be "done" once all the intraday bars have been seen. And your intraday bars have times which are greater than `00:00:00`.

`backtrader` has *human* limitations and believes that such a daily bar with a `00:00:00` time has already been closed and is therefore delivered with the intraday bar of the same time.

If you change the time of your daily bars to `23:59:59` the following output takes place:

```
1714: Minute 2016-02-09T00:00:00 Date 2016-02-08 14.7292
1715: Minute 2016-02-09T00:05:00 Date 2016-02-08 14.7292
1716: Minute 2016-02-09T00:10:00 Date 2016-02-08 14.7292
...
10528: Minute 2016-03-23T14:30:00 Date 2016-03-22 7.1464
10529: Minute 2016-03-23T14:35:00 Date 2016-03-22 7.1464
10530: Minute 2016-03-23T14:40:00 Date 2016-03-22 7.1464
```

With no error.

This is the only logical way to present the provided data. Looking at the last 3 lines

  - The intraday bars of 2016-03-23 are presented along with the last "closed" daily bar. Because 2016-03-23 is not yet over.

My educated guess is that what you are looking for is *data replay*. You want to see the intraday bars and how that's affecting the daily bar.

See the output produced by having `cerebro.replaydata(data0, timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Days)`. (where `data0` is the 5 minute input) instead of adding the daily data directly.

```
6: Minute 2016-02-08T01:35:00 Date 2016-02-08 11.5532
6: Minute 2016-02-08T02:15:00 Date 2016-02-08 11.8232
6: Minute 2016-02-08T03:40:00 Date 2016-02-08 12.0072
...
37: Minute 2016-03-23T13:35:00 Date 2016-03-23 8.9084
37: Minute 2016-03-23T13:45:00 Date 2016-03-23 9.0544
37: Minute 2016-03-23T13:55:00 Date 2016-03-23 9.2384
37: Minute 2016-03-23T14:05:00 Date 2016-03-23 9.2384
37: Minute 2016-03-23T14:20:00 Date 2016-03-23 9.2384
37: Minute 2016-03-23T14:30:00 Date 2016-03-23 9.2384
37: Minute 2016-03-23T14:40:00 Date 2016-03-23 9.2384
```

Now the intraday bars on 2016-03-23 have a counterpart (daily bar) with the same date. And the `ATR` is being recalculated for each 5 minutes bar on the daily bar. Obviously the last 5 price changes are non-existent and that's why the `ATR` remains constant.

If you wonder why the value is `9.2384` at the end of 2016-03-23 instead of your expected `8.5744`. Options:

  - The set of 5 minute bars misses bars which would change the `close`, `high`, or `low` prices of some daily bars (or contains too many, like pre-market/after-market)

  - The platform may be calculating things wrongly.

If instead of *replaying* the 5 minute bars we *resample* them to see if they are like the daily bars:

(add only 1 data to cerebro with `cerebro.resampledata(data0, timeframe=bt.timeFrame.Days)`)

```
00: date,open,high,low,close
01: 2016-02-01,190.75,199.52,182.75,196.32
02: 2016-02-02,196.35,196.94,180.23,183.20
03: 2016-02-03,184.29,184.90,170.18,173.58
...
35: 2016-03-21,233.00,239.88,232.74,238.50
36: 2016-03-22,238.50,238.99,232.56,233.00
37: 2016-03-23,234.50,235.20,220.38,221.00
```

And we look at the same days in the original `tsla` input:

```
datetime,open,high,low,close,volume,openinterest
2016-02-01,188.76,199.52,182.75,196.94,5297639,0
2016-02-02,192.42,193.12,180.23,182.78,5773637,0
2016-02-03,183.59,183.94,170.18,173.48,7931362,0
...
2016-03-21,235.34,239.88,235,238.32,5288985,0
2016-03-22,237.21,238.99,232.558,234.24,4300628,0
2016-03-23,232.37,234.73,222.03,222.58,4921013,0
```

We can immediately see that the *resampled* bars and the *daily* bars are not equal and hence the difference in the final value of the `ATR` above.

I would gladly blame the *resampling* code in `backtrader` but the 5 minute input for 2016-02-01 contains a 1st bar with an open value of `190.75` at 01:15:00. The 1st 5 minute bar with an open value of `188.76` is at 06:35:00.

The same `06:35:00` time applies when looking for an opening time which matches the daily input for the 2nd daily bar and also for the 3rd. So it is obvious that the 5 minute input actually contains too many bars.

The question here is if you want to compare inputs which actually carry a different data payload.

@jeanCarloMachado Thank you for your feedback, and feedback at any time is welcome.  To give some idea on timelines, the current full roadmap runs about a year out, and I aim for a major release with a couple big changes + several smaller feature additions & bugfixes every 3-6 months.

Between this and the other feedback, I've bumped the priority of xUnit compatible output up several notches, and it is planned for the next release.  The dynamic/flexible variable binding got bumped down in priority to allow for this. 

Logging is getting some extra love as well with https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/pull/171 and I'll factor in ways to improve the signal-to-noise ratio here.

Config files: I've added https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/177 to include this.  It depends on several planned features in future releases, so it will be a while out, but it's factored into design planning now.

Parallel: yes, I agree this would improve speed a lot.  It is a rather complex, multi-step process to get there, but here are the rough design plans. I'm open to ideas or examples if people have good samples that may help:

* Parallel execution depends on tests/benchmarks handling their own execution (and a resttest runner that is smart enough to fire them async). The first part is already about halfway there with https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/pull/171 and will get there within the next release.
* Using the [CurlMulti object instead of individual Curl handles](https://fragmentsofcode.wordpress.com/2011/01/22/pycurl-curlmulti-example/) allows for concurrent requests (and if we do requests compatibility, [they have something similar using gevent](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/v0.10.6/user/advanced/). 
* It's *probably* easiest to implement parallel execution a batch at a time, rather than true async, initially.
* Unfortunately, the dynamic variable binding mechanism (variables/generators/extractors/validators/templating) will cause problems if tests are not executed in the correct order.
  - For this reason, v1 will be something crude, where you have to explicitly mark testsets or tests for parallel execution.  Only sequential, parallelizable tests will be run in parallel (to preserve correctness).  
  -  Then we worry about tracking what can safely run in parallel and automatically doing so.  
    - v2 of the feature would only run where no generator/variable re-binding/extractor binding is being used (all configuration uses an unchanged Context). 
    - v3 would evaluate predictable variable bindings before each test and pass the results in, then fall back to serial execution where extractors are being used at all.
    - v4 would look at *which* variables are changing, and create a directed acyclic graph of variable modifications, and only disable parallel where variables are changing in unpredictable ways.

This is to be expected and unfortunately there isn't much I can do about it. If you're using `local_js=1`, the Javascript resources required to display the page are read from local disk. This is required if you're opening the cmdb using a `file://cmdb.html` link, because modern browser do not allow you to load http/https resources from a `file://` origin. In your case, you're moving the files to a host that doesn't have the required javascript libraries because ansible-cmdb isn't installed.

The proper solution (if you don't want to install ansible-cmdb on the computer you're trying to view the output on) is to use `local_js=0` and host the resulting html file(s) on a webserver. This way, it will be using the `http://` or `https://` origin, and it can load javascript sources from a CDN. You could host the output on a Apache or Nginx server, or (if Python is installed) you can use Python to spin up a simple webserver:

    $ ls cmdb.html  # generated with -p local_js=0
    cmdb.html
    $ python3 -m http.server   # Use "-m SimpleHTTPServer" for python v2
    Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8000 (http://0.0.0.0:8000/) ...
    $ xdg-open http://0.0.0.0:8000/cmdb.html
    Created new window in existing browser session.

Follow these steps for easiest way to finetuning the only last layer:

1) Open your vggface.py source code and find the layer you want to customize.
2) Change the parameters and name of that layer. 
3) Find the model.load_weights(path) function in the source code for your suitable backend and dimension ordering and change it to model.load_weights(path, by_name=True).
4) Freeze other layers like you mentioned.
5) Start training.

I investigated and understood the bug.

I can now confirm, the issue is indeed with `django.template.loaders.cached.Loader` — on the second `Template.render` invocation `self.kwargs` are already rewritten with resolved values from the previous `render` call.

Here's the essence of the issue:

```python
t = Template(
    "{% load macros %}"
    "{% macro test kwarg=foo %}{{ kwarg }}{% endmacro %}"
    "{% use_macro test %}"
)
c1 = Context({"foo": "one"})
c2 = Context({"foo": "two"})
assert t.render(c1) != t.render(c2), "The results must be different"
```

My originally proposed fix was completely wrong. The correct solution is to not do anything to `self.kwargs` in `DefineMacroNode.render`, but to store everything in independent variable. Then, use that variable in `UseMacroNode.render` instead of `self.macro.kwargs`. E.g. something along the lines of:

```diff
--- a/test_project_python27/test_project_python27/macros/templatetags/macros.py
+++ b/test_project_python27/test_project_python27/macros/templatetags/macros.py
@@ -52,8 +52,8 @@ class DefineMacroNode(template.Node):
         # variables.
         #
         # recall all defaults are template variables
-        self.kwargs = {k: v.resolve(context)
-                       for k, v in self.kwargs.items()}
+        self.kwresolved = {k: v.resolve(context)
+                           for k, v in self.kwargs.items()}
 
         # empty string - {% macro %} tag has no output
         return ''
@@ -202,7 +202,7 @@ class UseMacroNode(template.Node):
                 context[arg] = ""
 
         # add all of use_macros kwargs into context
-        for name, default in self.macro.kwargs.items():
+        for name, default in self.macro.kwresolved.items():
             if name in self.kwargs:
                 context[name] = self.kwargs[name].resolve(context)
             else:
```

I just bumped into this error on OSX 10.10 using `pip install --user pyscard`.

This is related to swig/swig#250. In other words, for newer versions of Swig the generated `smartcard/scard/scard,py` is different and not backward compatible to files generated by previous versions of Swig. Thus, `smartcard/scard/scard,py` should be re-generated too before swig is invoked during installation.

Ok, it seems like you're attempting to do two things here:

* make it so parameters can be entered through csv

I suggest instead writing a wrapper around twitterscraper which calls it based on a csv files parameters, e.g.

```
from twitterscraper import query_tweets
with open(csvfilename) as f:
    rows = csv.reader(f)

    for row in rows:
        query = 'from:' + row[1]
        res = query_tweets(query)
        # play with res here
```

### Raspberry Pi speed

The face_encodings() method runs a feed-forward pass on a deep neural network, which is basically all matrix multiplication. 

Modern Intel CPUs have support for SSE4/AVX SIMD instructions which parallelize that math in the CPU for a big speed boost. The RPi's ARM cpu doesn't support SSE instructions. ARM has it's own SIMD feature called NEON but unfortunately dlib doesn't currently support NEON so it doesn't take advantage of SIMD to speed things up. That's why it's so slow on RPi.

Someone made an unofficial dlib patch that uses NEON instructions on ARM, but it's totally unsupported and probably doesn't work for face recognition unless someone did some more work on it: https://github.com/fastfastball/dlib_for_arm

There's also an open issue on dlib asking for ARM NEON support, but it hasn't been implemented yet: https://github.com/davisking/dlib/issues/276

So unless you are able to contribute to dlib in this area, unfortunately the best short-term answer is to just reduce the resolution of the image as low as you can to keep the face recognition times reasonable on RPi. Hopefully it will get better soon!

### Re-training, etc

This library uses [dlib](http://dlib.net/).  You can use dlib directly if you need more flexibility.  You can see more info about re-training here on the dlib blog: http://blog.dlib.net/2017/02/high-quality-face-recognition-with-deep.html

So basically your argument is you don't trust a project's declaration that it supports Python 3 without validating all dependencies also make that declaration? The problem with that is you're going to end up with a ton of false negatives from projects that have not declared Python 3 support but which are implicitly declared by dependent projects. For example, if project A doesn't declare Python 3 support but project B does and has verified things work, then why deal with the false negative result of project A being flagged as not supporting Python 3 when project B has already taken care of that for us implicitly.

This would also place a great burden on this project to make the overrides file comprehensive which would be never-ending. There are too many projects on PyPI which are dormant or make too infrequent of releases to fix their Trove classifiers to make a thorough check succeed without a ton of projects getting added to the overrides file and I simply don't want to deal with that workload.

As for the point of using caniusepython3 on a project that prematurely declared Python 3 support, my answer is to just not prematurely declare Python 3 support. 😉 The Python 3 Trove classifier should simply not be added to a project until support has been reached; classifiers are not aspirational goals but statements about the state of the code.

So while I understand your motivations for wanting a way to perform a more complete check, I still think in the general case it's a YAGNI feature and it will put an undue workload on this project that I don't think is beneficial enough for what it will lead to.

I created https://github.com/brettcannon/caniusepython3/blob/master/README_PyPI.rst#integrating-with-your-tests to handle the test integration case originally.

I see now; thanks. I believe the only references to `request.session` are in the callback functions that handle setting or deleting cookies. I haven't worked through this part in a while but I think there are conditions where the `request` that gets passed into the cookie callback could be different/modified from the time the callback itself is created.

For instance, the `_cookie_callback` function's use of `request.session` (https://github.com/ericrasmussen/pyramid_redis_sessions/blob/master/pyramid_redis_sessions/__init__.py#L302-L315) needs to see if the current session has been invalidated. This is to prevent a cookie from being set if the session becomes invalidated at any point. The tricky part is when we eventually add this function as a request callback (https://github.com/ericrasmussen/pyramid_redis_sessions/blob/master/pyramid_redis_sessions/__init__.py#L234-L241) it has to be able to get to the session via the request, and the only pyramid-supported way of doing that is accessing `request.session`.

This isn't something I'm up to addressing right now but if you want to contribute a fix I'm definitely open to it. I'm sure there's a way to refactor it to depend on elements of the request not in the session (maybe using `request.registry`), or if all else fails, adding a new override function (see https://github.com/ericrasmussen/pyramid_redis_sessions/blob/master/pyramid_redis_sessions/__init__.py#L79-L82 for examples) to obtain the session from a request. The default function would be something like `lambda request: request.session`, and you'd be able to pass in your own there to get around it.

There might be some other benefits to the latter idea (allowing an override function for obtaining the current session), including being able to specify your own callback in your `ini` file and far less work to maintain backwards compatibility.

Sort of... the best way to explain is to use [this modified mnist_spark.py snippet](https://gist.github.com/leewyang/7a44844771ebc96f4344ab7a49a74637), which tries to run train immediately followed by test, but without a clean `shutdown` in between.

Under normal operation, the `reserve` step will run on distinct executors, assigning one TF node per executor.  Note that we have the `raise Exception` in the original code to help ensure this.  The `start` step will then start the TF processes, one per-executor, but in the background.  The worker tasks will complete and free up the executors, but the PS nodes will block the task completion by waiting for a control signal.  For RDD feeding, we then use SparkContext's threading capability to spawn a new feeding job, which will not assign any of the data feeding tasks to the still-busy executor (hosting the PS process).  When the data feeding completes, we use the `shutdown` step to notify the blocked PS task to finally exit, which completes the initial `start` job.

Now, if we don't invoke the `shutdown` cleanly (per the gist), the PS task will continue to tie up one executor.  So, if we follow up with another `reserve`, Spark will now try to assign the N reserve tasks on N-1 nodes.  It will throw various `TFManager already started` exceptions, but it will eventually complete because of Spark's Python worker pools.  However, we end up with two TF nodes (e.g. worker + ps) assigned to the same executor.  And, in my tests, the PS node reserved last, so it now "owns" the `TFManager` for that executor.  Subsequently, when we try to `start` both TF nodes on this problematic executor, it actually starts the worker first (because we actually had the [PS nodes wait a bit on startup](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/examples/mnist/spark/mnist_dist.py#L30-L32)).  And when we start feeding data into it, it connects to the local TFManager (which now has PS queues only), and fails with the `KeyError`.

```python

UTF-8 will not always be the right encoding, especially on Windows, but if it's mostly ascii characters you can get away with it.

Now, that was simple.

What's more complicated is to make `Foo2` a subclass of `Configurable`, 
and implicitly modify values, by loading *configurations* into the "hierarchy".
For this to work, you have to set the `parent` property.  

A more descriptive code is given below to exemplify this case:

```python
from traitlets import *
from traitlets.config import *
   
class Bar(Configurable):
    num = Int().tag(config=True)

class Foo(Configurable):
    mybar = Instance(Bar).tag(config=True)

## Build the configuration
#
c = Config()
c.Foo.Bar.num = 3  # The more specific value applies.
c.Bar.num = 1      # Would apply if above was missing.

## Build and configure the hierarchy.
#
f = Foo(config=c)
f.mybar = Bar(parent=f)
assert f.mybar.num == 3
```

Note that child-assignment must happen AFTER parent has loaded config:
```python
f = Foo()
f.mybar = Bar(parent=f)
f.config = c    # Too late to apply to child.
assert f.mybar.num == 0
```

...or else, you need to apply it specifically to child:
```python
f = Foo()
f.mybar = Bar(parent=f)
f.mybar.config = c
assert f.mybar.num == 3
```
..of course you can set your code to *observe* the `config` param and update child's `config`,
but we are entering too deep into details now.

```
conn = S3Connection(a,b)
bucket = conn.get_bucket(bucket)
key = bucket.get_key(key_path)
byte_block = key.get_contents_as_string(headers={'Range': 'bytes=22000-23000'})
```

When looking at the code-path taken by fetch/pull, it becomes apparent that it will [enforce opening stdout](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/7b854878fb9df8d1a06c4e97bff5e164957b3a0d/git/remote.py#L653), even though it [never reads from it](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/7b854878fb9df8d1a06c4e97bff5e164957b3a0d/git/remote.py#L553) - this could be the deadlock you experience.

literally every community calls it locking, so it makes sense to call it a lockfile. pipenv is already using this, and it works well.

I strongly suggest not to make `Pipfile` a Python file, nor a subset of Python, parsed by a specific DSL-parser. making it a pure-Python file opens the  door to all these hacks we already have in the `setup.py` field in Python (it might have been a good idea when conceived, but it lead to so many problems. Let https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/distutils.html be a hint). Using a subset of Python as a DSL feels like reinventing the wheel and will mean that I as a user of python packaging tools have to learn a DSL that is surely similar like Python, but subtly different. And I won't be able to apply this to any other project.

I am convinced that Pypa should look into Rust's `Cargo.toml` as an inspiration. Rust's packaging infrastructure is not "right by accident", but by careful research and design. Then, either use, TOML, YAML or ConfigParser formats, but please do not roll your own DSL or make it a programmatic Python file.

Or at least some of them would be useful.  I think the most compelling features are hooks--both hooks that allow modifying the metadata read from the config, and pre/post command hooks.

Less compelling features (that were originally brought over from distutils2) might be the ability to specify new custom compiler classes.  I don't think this has every been used--very few projects provide their own custom Compiler classes, and those that do (e.g. Numpy) have other huge piles of custom build code written on top of distutils...

Re-reading your question and thinking about it some more, here's what I recommend. 

The most direct and best way to determine if a version number is valid is to simply parse it with `packaging.version.Version`, which will raise an exception if it's invalid.

But if you're currently relying on the interfaces supplied by setuptools/pkg_resources, the best way to determine if a version is valid may be something like this:

```
ver = pkg_resources.parse_version(input)
is_legacy = 'Legacy' in ver.__class__.__name__
```

That approach will work for old and future versions of pkg_resources for as long as I can forsee.

@RonnyPfannschmidt He is not the only one working on `setuptools`, but in any case it's important to get the *right* decision.

In fact, I think it would be much *worse* if `distutils` moved into `setuptools` and there was only one person who doesn't have much time to work on it maintaining the project. CPython has a long release cadence but also has many contributors.

Most of the `setuptools` API is already subclassing and/or patching the base `distutils` classes as it is. I think we can start moving over those classes in their entirety rather than subclassing, and implement the patches directly in `setuptools`.

The main problems I could foresee this causing are people doing `isinstance` checks against `distutils` and people possibly accidentally relying on the `setuptools` monkey patches. Both of these may be fixable (at least to the degree that they are fixed by the "move distutils into the setuptools repo" solution) with a deprecation period.

Currently PutObject() calculates md5sum for each part while uploading to remote server we need to change this behavior into two parts 

- For a fresh upload we do not calculate md5sum for each parts, instead we rely on the fact that server anyways calculates and we keep uploading. 
- For a resuming upload i.e resuming a previously interrupted upload should calculate md5sum of each parts and skip necessary parts. (This is an acceptable scenario)
- For a resuming upload we can also avoid calculating md5sum for the parts that are not uploaded yet.

Hi @vdboor thanks for the comments and questions. Sorry for the late reply, the code branch has been in a lot of flux and I wanted to get it (somewhat) more stable before replying.

Based on your feedback I have updated the integration to automatically support versioning by modifying the relevant admin classes to extend the integration versions if `reversion` is installed and enabled. I tried to do this in minimally intrusive way, and it works but is fairly hacky. [For example](https://github.com/ixc/django-fluent-pages/blob/22880bfde335a126852c55c551c0d8bde90d70a7/fluent_pages/adminui/urlnodeparentadmin.py#L180)

* Does this code already work with the latest django-reversion?  
  It uses the latest django-reversion except for one outstanding pending fix for polymorphic page links, see https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/pull/438 The maintainer of django-reversion [Dave Hall](https://github.com/etianen) has been super-helpful in merging and updating the library to work better in complex situations like for fluent pages

* Automatically mix in reversion support and avoid requiring the `FLUENT_PAGES_PARENT_ADMIN_MIXIN` setting  
  Done

* Is there ever a reason to disable reversion support for pages when the rest of your project runs on reversion?  
  Maybe not. I can imagine situations where some parts of a site use `reversion` but you don't want fluent pages to do so, though it's unlikely. I would like to keep the `FLUENT_PAGES_DISABLE_REVERSION` optional setting for now at least, as it provides a quick way to disable the versioning support to see whether or not it is causing a specific issue

* The `ReversionRealtimeSignalProcessor` seems like a workaround. Can this be fixed for real, or should this be upstreamed to django-parler?  
  The term "workaround" is too kind, it's more of a hack. It would be good to fix it for real, though I'm not sure of the best way to do this. The problem may actually have been solved by recent changes in django-reversion to do strict revert/restore operations – I haven't had a chance to re-test

* If this feature doesn't need to be in INSTALLED_APPS, it would make sense to upstream it to django-fluent-utils, so it can be integrated into django-fluent-blogs and django-fluent-faq too.  
  That sounds reasonable. It isn't something I will have time to take on in the near-term, but once it is working well it will hopefully be possible to relocate the bulk of the code.

* What is the relation with django-model-publisher? It seems like a nice replacement of the default status field.  
  We are actually using the integration along with django-model-publisher though with a lot of code customisations. That could make for another good integration project in the future.

* btw, if you have some sites running on django-fluent, I'd love to add those to https://django-fluent.org/showcase/ :)  
  Heh, nothing that's publicly visible just yet but that sounds like a good idea for when the site eventually goes live.

Returning to the naming question, for those of us with intermittent repetitive stress injuries, _minimal characters_ is a crucial design choice. If autocomplete is available this is less of a problem, but to my knowledge this isn't true for colormap names.

Thus, 'pastelrocket', 'softsunrise', etc. are _far, far too long_. Almost any mashup of two words will be.

Seeing as we have perfectly valid names at 4-6 characters (ignis, magma, lava, rocket, ...), I would be disappointed to see that figure more than double. Seven or eight is OK, more is too much.

The trouble with 'nebula' is that nebulas only look like these colormaps in a small subset of false-color images, and the actual "color" content in those images is extremely variable. Nebula as a category doesn't speak to me as any particular color scheme, but that's me.

Finally, in the vein of 'rocket': **'scramjet'** is a bit longer but has a delicious double entendre...

> You mention a different NDK? What should that be?

I just mean the latest NDK release from https://developer.android.com/ndk/downloads/ , currently r17. The main change is likely to be the need to move to clang, I don't expect anything else in particular to break.

> Lastly,with regards to gcc vs. clang, don't know much but am willing to learn if you can point me to some docs on changes that will need to be made, which version I need to install etc.

On p4a's side, it currently chooses a gcc toolchain to use, and uses the path to that later in the build process. It does this [here](https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/blob/master/pythonforandroid/build.py#L414). I think this needs to be changed to look for the clang toolchain instead.

Beyond that, I would basically proceed by trying to change it and seeing what breaks. I don't have a deep idea about what will need doing. There is some clang documentation at https://developer.android.com/ndk/guides/standalone_toolchain#working_with_clang , and I've found before that since many projects have had to make the same change there are plenty of questions already asked online about the problems that come up.

Just to note, while this does need doing, NDK r16 should actually work currently using gcc. I don't think this is going to break in the near future, unlike targeting older apis, although moving to clang is obviously still important.

This monkey patch covers my example and the ones listed in the function signature, but i am not sure what other use cases this function is intended to cover:

```python
def norm_url2(base, url):
    """
    >>> norm_url('http://example.org/', '/one')
    'http://example.org/one'
    >>> norm_url('http://example.org/', '/one#')
    'http://example.org/one#'
    >>> norm_url('http://example.org/one', 'two')
    'http://example.org/two'
    >>> norm_url('http://example.org/one/', 'two')
    'http://example.org/one/two'
    >>> norm_url('http://example.org/', 'http://example.net/one')
    'http://example.net/one'
    >>> norm_url('http://example.org/', 'http://example.net#one')
    'http://example.net#one'
    """

    result = urljoin(base, url)
    if url.endswith('#') and not result.endswith('#'):
        result += '#'
    return result

from rdflib_jsonld import util
util.norm_url = norm_url2
```

ok, looking through your most recent changes, these have made things a mixture of better and worse and its probably my fault for not explaining adequately. I have created three bullet points below to summarize the salient information. If you want me to turn this into examples, please let me know.

1. `aria-hidden="true"` will hide all content and semantics and is the technique that should be used to hide unwanted unicode characters
1. `title` attribute should only be used as an accessible name on interactive elements like links or buttons, in all other cases, `sr-only` should be used in addition to title to support users with cognitive disabilities (see additional information below)
1. role="presentation" could be used on the `<i>` tags in cases where the unicode character is meaningful. This is really a "futureproofing" best practice because most screen reader settings do not cause the semantics of that tag to be exposed (but they could).

Additional Information: the title attribute is part of the ARIA name calculation algorithm as the "fallback". ARIA name calculation, while well supported for well known role combinations, is not supported by any browser+AT combinations in its entirety. So for example, using aria-label on a `<div>` will not work on most platforms, while using it on an interactive element like a button or a link, will work everywhere. The same applies to the title attribute. Therefore, title will provide the most bang for the buck when used on an interactive element, where it can serve as the accessible name, the visible hint and the target for Dragon. When used on non-interactive elements, it is really only serving the purpose of a hint which helps users with cognitive disabilities.

FWIW, this example from the docs is part of a tested snippet:

```python
>>> from google.cloud import pubsub
>>> import os
>>> publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()
>>> topic = 'projects/{project_id}/topics/{topic}'.format(
...     project_id=os.getenv('GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT'),
...     topic='MY_TOPIC_NAME',  # Set this to something appropriate.
... )
>>> publisher.create_topic(topic)  # raises conflict if topic exists
>>> publisher.publish(topic, b'My first message!', spam='eggs')
```

Note that the message payload (the bytes literal) is passed as a positional argument, but the message attribute (`spam`) is passed as a keyword argument.  If you have an existing dictionary of message attributes, you can pass it through via Python's `**` syntax, e.g.

```python
>>> publisher.publish(topic, b'My first message!', **message_attributes)
```

I like the suggestion that Pants throw an error if it doesn't see the patterns it's looking for. At one point, that was the behavior. It's good for catching these problems.

One thing to be careful of using marker-comments instead of snippets from the code itself: if pants greps for patterns in the code itself, then changing the code horks up an error... and probably draws your attention to some docs that need updating. (This isn't infallible: and if I find myself updating a pattern a couple of times w/out changing the surrounding docs, I remember that--and switch over to using comments.) If instead it's looking for comments, then doc-generation doesn't break so often; but you're more likely to have docs drift out of touch with the code they describe.

to be clear, I think the `ImportError: No module named 'setuptools'` problem is a WONTFIX because that's how it would also work outside of pants. injecting our own setuptools in this case would be the wrong thing to do as far as I'm concerned because it's implicit hardcoding. hardcoding would work only in this case because the dep is setuptools and so is the requirement, but what about when it's something else and the user assumes we cover all setup.py declared `setup_requires` deps when we actually don't?

Regarding the MS-API for the Bookmarks collection:

* `.Application` and `.Creator` are not applicable. `.Creator` could *possibly* be used, like somehow assigning an identifier specific to `python-docx`, but we've never done that before and I don't see the compelling use case for it so far.
* `Bookmarks.Count` maps to `len(Bookmarks)`, of course.
* On sorting, I'm not seeing the immediate need for that. I'd be inclined to just skip that as someone who needed it could easily do it themselves. Bookmark objects would already appear in document order; if they wanted them by name that would be two or three lines of client code for the caller.
* `.Parent` we can skip as the parent is always the `Document` object so far, which they just used to get the `Bookmarks` object.
* `.ShowHidden` I don't think we can do because we don't track hidden parts of the document (and I'm not exactly sure what hidden status actually is).

As for the methods:

* `.Add()` we can't do because we don't have ranges. We'll have the `.start_bookmark()` and `.end_bookmark()` methods folks can use for this job (or whatever we end up deciding on).
* `.Exists()` - I'm not sure of the semantics for this one, but if it's just finding out if a bookmark with a certain name exists, folks can do that easily enough with something like:
  ```python
  bookmark = Bookmarks.get(name)
  if bookmark is None:
      # do False thing
  else:
      # do True thing
* `.Item()` is just indexed access.

So in short, I think we're good with a `Bookmarks` object that has the behaviors I outlined in the last bullet of the previous comment.

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

""" Dump all known UPnP commands """

from __future__ import unicode_literals

from pprint import pprint

from soco import SoCo, discover, services

# get a zone player - any one will do
z1 = discover().pop()
print "Querying %s" % z1.player_name
# get an instance of each service
services = (srv(z1) for srv in services.Service.__subclasses__())
for srv in services:
    name = srv.service_type
    box = "="*79
    print "{0}\n|{1:^77}|\n{0}\n".format(box, name)
    for action in srv.iter_actions():
        print action.name
        print "~"*len(action.name)
        print "\n  Input"
        for arg in action.in_args:
            print "    ", arg
        print "\n  Output"
        for arg in action.out_args:
            print "    ", arg
        print "\n\n"

> In both the ipwb replay and indexer we use the cat() function for IPFS via the ipfsapi module to obtain data from ipfs within Python.

The `pullFromIPFS` method defined in the indexer (that uses `ipfsapi.cat` method) is not used anywhere. This is a dead code and should be removed.

> Would it be better to use ipfsapi.get()? Can we .get() and return the data from a function?

We are using the right method. To retrieve the data in the form of a string (hence in memory) [ipfsapi.cat](https://ipfs.io/ipns/QmZ86ow1byeyhNRJEatWxGPJKcnQKG7s51MtbHdxxUddTH/Software/Python/ipfsapi/api_ref.html#ipfsapi.Client.cat) is used. corresponding native IPFS CLI `cat` yields the content on STDOUT. On the other hand, [ipfsapi.get](https://ipfs.io/ipns/QmZ86ow1byeyhNRJEatWxGPJKcnQKG7s51MtbHdxxUddTH/Software/Python/ipfsapi/api_ref.html#ipfsapi.Client.get) would download the file in the current working directory and will return nothing useful which is not desired in this case.

The problem is your controller is running v2.0.0, and the first release of libjuju was built against v2.0.1. In that release, the Application facade version was incremented (to version 3). In other words, the client (libjuju) needs v3 of that facade, but the server is only advertising v1 and v2.

Basically you need to upgrade your controller. 

There is an issue for improving our version handling here: https://github.com/juju/python-libjuju/issues/49

- If you run `conda create -n test -c jakirkham scipy openblas`, it chooses the wrong version of scipy. This is caused by https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1338, and was already reported at https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1334.

- If you run `conda create -n test -c jakirkham openblas` and then `conda install -n test -c jakirkham scipy`, it doesn't install scipy. Ok, I've further improved the version handling to move it as close to PEPs 386/440 as possible:
* string comparisons are now case-insensitive
* epochs are handled correctly

A few differences remain:
* PEP 440 treats `c` and `rc` as identical, whereas the proposed code doesn't. Thus, `1.1.1c1 == 1.1.1rc1` in PEP 440, but `1.1.1c1 < 1.1.1rc1` in my code. In think it is best to handle this case by requiring _self-consistent_ version numbering, i.e. any given package must stick to either `c` or `rc`. The documentation should be extended to say so explicitly.
* PEP 440 allows a separator to be inserted before the prerelease letter, so that `1.1-a1 == 1.1_a1 == 1.1a1 == 1.1.a1`. In contrast, the present proposal has `1.1-a1 < 1.1_a1 < 1.1a1 < 1.1.a1`. Again, this is best handled by requiring self-consistency.
* The present proposal doesn't ignore a leading `v`. I don't see any justification for silently treating `v1.1 == 1.1`.
* PEP 440 requires special treatment of the string `.post` which signifies post-release versions. In contrast to normal string components, `post` is considered to be greater than any number. I suggest that the documentation should say explicitly that this special behavior is only available for conforming version strings.

BTW, the documentation should be extended to explain the difference between conforming and non-conforming version strings to begin with.

Finally, there is no way to design a PEP 440 compatible versioning scheme that handles 'openssl' correctly, because 'openssl' occasionally comes with conforming version strings but requires them to be ordered differently. I therefore added explicit special treatment of 'openssl'.

Please remember that this PR is not meant to solve the versioning problem once and for all (this is beyond my competence and time constraints). I just want to improve the fall-back for non-conforming version strings.

> Moreover, as I showed yesterday, directly using conda install gnuplot still correctly finds and installs gnuplot-5.0.3-3. This is at the very best inconsistent behavior that should be documented.

This is actually easy to explain. `conda`'s first priority is installing the latest versions of the packages that you specify on the command line; everything else is secondary. So of course this is going to get you the latest version of `gnuplot`.

But if gnuplot is just a *dependency* of another package, it has to balance installing the latest compatible versions of it versus all of the *other* dependencies it's pulling in. I'm going to hazard a guess that there is a tradeoff here between your "older" versions of `libgd` and the "older" versions of `gnuplot`, and `4.0.4` is making a different choice. Given the more clear-cut issues with version preferences we've had to fix in the last dot-0 release, I'm not surprised.

Oh, it'd be very nice to have it exactly the same, but that will be a lot more coding than I'm willing to add right now.  Here's why:  [Date math support in _index_ names](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/6.1/date-math-index-names.html) (emphasis mine).

Elasticsearch does the work for this for the `create_index` action.  That's why it works there.  The problem with adding this for snapshot names is that it isn't supported by Elasticsearch, so I'd have to mimic the behavior _exactly,_ or risk upsetting users who expect identical behavior.  I've had my eye on adding this behavior for a while, but it's very difficult to make it happen perfectly, as the libraries and such for Elasticsearch are in Java, and Curator is in Python.

As of [0.32.0](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/releases/tag/v0.32.0) the library supports automatic calculation of transactional MD5s for upload and download. Transactional MD5 is more versatile than stored MD5 as it is computed by the server on the actual stored data. It does not have the issue where the stored content MD5 can get out of date as the blob is updated. Simply use the validate_content MD5 to enable this for the upload of any blob type and blob download. Note that using MD5 validation has a performance penalty and HTTPS already protects against bitflips on the wire so this feature should be used primarily if HTTP is used.

The library does not build in calculation of the total blob MD5 or validate it on download as this historically in other libraries causes issues with the stored MD5 being out of date with the content. However, the user may still set and retrieve this value using the content_settings. Note that by default stored MD5 not returned by the download APIs unless particular provisions are made as described in the get method docs. It can still be retrieved by getting the blob properties.

To me, if the same error code can be associated with significantly different error messages, then something is wrong with the design of the source code. An error code should always mean the same thing. For example, "file not found" might be the message for an error code, and the specific file might vary, but I wouldn't want the same error code to also mean "file permission error". We need the ability to have a KB article about each error code, to be able to seek help with an error code on stack overflow, etc.

If there is consensus about what I'm asserting (and there might not be; I'm curious...), then the only potential variation between error messages for a given code would be the specific context. For example, a "JSON parsing error" with code 123 might sometimes be associated with a missing curly brace at position 100, and sometimes with a missing quote mark at position 1000, but it would always be a JSON parsing error.

If that's true, then I think the value of the specific context is debatable. It's nice to return highly specific error messages, and as a programmer I have appreciated them when APIs have provided them. However, I'm not sure it's worth a significant complication to the API to achieve this; most of the time, the programmer can infer or recreate the context once they know the general problem. If it's a "file not found" error, the programmer can figure out which file is missing, and if it's a JSON parsing error, the programmer can parse the JSON independently to discover what's wrong.

The reason I'm pushing back is that I don't think there's a convenient way to return both an error code and a message from every API. If you can think of a clean way to do so, that doesn't add a lot of complication for the caller and the callee, let's explore...

Actually, casting breaks on sufficiently large numbers. `int(x)`, where `x` is a `long`, returns a `long` value if `x` is greater than the max possible value of a 32-bit integer. The right way to do this would be to check that the type is in `six.integer_types` and, if so, to then do a bounds check on the value, to make sure that it's a valid, signed 32-bit integer value.

Thanks for advice! Was playing with properties today, here's what i've came up with:

Had to implement custom `get_values()`, as `vars(self)` can't show the property, it shows private variable instead.
I thought it was already a working solution, as i tried to test it with some pre-defined values like this:
```python
host = entities.Host(
            location=1,
            organization=1,
            owner=3,
            owner_type='Usergroup',
).create()
```
and it was working ok, but when i set `owner=` to a Usergroup enitity - no success.
The reason is following:
When we initialize new Host() instance, we have `self._fields['owner']` set to `OneToOneField(User)` by default. When it comes to populating the instance with values provided as arguments, in `Entity.__init__()` we're going through all the arguments provided. Unfortunately, `owner` comes before `owner_type`.
That means we have `owner == entity.UserGroup()`, but `self._fields['owner'] == entity.User()`.
As a result, in `Entity.__init__()` we call for `_make_entity_from_id()` to receive an entity with its id. And that function checks, if the same entity as in `self._fields` declaration was provided. If so - it fetches the id, otherwise it assumes that probably id, not the entity was provided and just sets provided value to id field.
So we're recieving `entities.Host().owner` set to `entities.User` instance (not `entities.Usergroup` as we're expecting), and even more - that `owner.id` is not integer value, but another entity - `entities.Usergroup` :smile: 
Any ideas on how to avoid that?

My suggestion is to use pytest-cov's append mode and then on the remote side (docker or whatever it is) you can either:
A) - Run your code with coverage (see https://coverage.readthedocs.io/ for how to activate)
B) - Run your code with pytest-cov's engine (if you need the subprocess handling and such). For this you only need to set 3 env vars:
  
  - COV_CORE_SOURCE=src or whatever
  - COV_CORE_CONFIG=path/to/.coveragerc
  - COV_CORE_DATAFILE=path/to/.coverage.somesuffix

Note that the suffix is necessary if you don't have unique hostnames.

Also, your `.coveragerc` should at least have:
```
[run]
parallel = true 
```

SourceMap encodes similar information to PDB. Since it's just JSON format it can presumably be extended and store any additional information that managed compilers need to store in PDB beyond what's specified by https://sourcemaps.info/spec.html. We could have used SourceMap. That said, the Portable PDB format is more compact and it's based on the managed metadata format that managed compilers already produce. It's optimized for both size and speed of access to various information based on what the common needs of managed debuggers are.

You can find the specification here: https://github.com/dotnet/corefx/blob/master/src/System.Reflection.Metadata/specs/PortablePdb-Metadata.md for comparison with SourceMap.

OK, I've given this some more thought, and I don't think this should be merged as is because of the way I have `fallback_on_invalid_day`'s deafults set. Right now, without the smart parser turned on, "Feb 30, 2014" and "Feb 2014" with a default date of "January 30th, 2014" are dealt with the same way, even though the first one is actually an invalid date and the second one should arguably fall back to the end of the month (as I believe `relativedelta` does).

The right solution, I think, is to make `fallback_on_invalid_day` a trinary - if it's unspecified or `None`, then the fallback behavior would be in effect only if the reason for the invalid parse is that the day is actually missing.

I won't worry about invalid dates created because of missing years - for example "Feb 29" with a default date in 2014 probably *should* throw an error by default, since there's reason to believe that the parser is giving you a date other than the one that was specified.

I think `QuantizedTensor` should be always initialized and immutable (except for `copy`, `set` and `release`). It can have three types of constructor:

1. `QuantizedTensor(tensor, desc)` - which creates a QuantizedTensor from a normal tensor
2. `QuantizedTensor(shape, desc)` - which creates a QuantizedTensor with native storage only
3. `QuantizedTensor(bytes, max, min, sum, desc)` - which creates a QuantizedTensor from the serialized data

I think we should remove the `init`, `setStorage` and `resize` methods from `QuantizedTensor`

> OK, but now that we're using `MANIFEST.in`; how come we're not able to exclude the `conftest.py` file?

Because it can only be used to completely exclude it from the source distribution. It does not allow to control which files are installed (except via `include_package_data`, which tells setuptools to install everything from MANIFEST.in). From the source distribution, files can only be chosen to be installed  in terms of Python packages, and `conftest.py` is part of the lasagne package.

Yeah I get the same problem. But, that's because this code is modifying NWBFile class definition.

deepcopy does not copy class attributes, so this line:
```python
MockNWBFile.__init__ = partialmethod(NWBFile.__init__,
                                     source='source',
                                     session_description='session_description',
                                     identifier='identifier',
                                     session_start_time=datetime.now(),
                                     file_create_date=datetime.now(),
                                     institution='institution',
                                     lab='lab')
```
is actually overwriting the \_\_init\_\_ method that's defined in NWBFile. If you want a NWBFile instance with a modified constructor, just subclass NWBFile.

I suspect that this isn't the only place that the hard association between the NWBFile object that is loaded and the backend might bite a user.

I could also imagine a "copy" method on NWBFile that loads the full file into memory and breaks any existing association with the source file. This gives you a "clean" object, like you just created it from scratch & writing it anew works as expected.

For the record, when setting up pyup you have choices. If you split up your dependencies as `requirements/default.txt` and `requirements/constraints.txt` you can tell PyUp to only bother checking `requirements/default.txt` and (I've never done this but I assume it's possible) only security alerts on `requirements/constraints.txt`. Then you don't get flooded with pyup pull requests for "trivial" packages like `requests`, `six` or `botocore`. 

I failed to do this with [Tecken](https://github.com/mozilla-services/tecken/blob/master/requirements.txt) so now I get [PRs](https://github.com/mozilla-services/tecken/pulls?q=is%3Apr+is%3Aopen+sort%3Aupdated-desc) for all sorts of "unimportant" packages.

I propose that we not move this code to `pulp_smash.tests.pulp2.utils`. The reason is that we're likely going to blow away the `pulp_smash.tests` directory when splitting tests out into separate repositories, as per #949.

Instead, I propose that we move these utilities to `pulp_smash.pulp2.utils`. This would be part of building the following file structure:

```console
$ tree -I tests pulp_smash
pulp_smash
├── api.py
├── cli.py
├── config.py
├── constants.py
├── exceptions.py
├── __init__.py
├── pulp2
│   ├── constants.py
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── utils.py
├── pulp3
│   ├── constants.py
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── utils.py
├── pulp_smash_cli.py
├── selectors.py
└── utils.py
```

An alternative plan could be to create files at `pulp_smash/constants/pulp{2,3}.py`, `pulp_smash/utils/pulp{2,3}.py`, and so on. But I think that namespaces are cleanest with the solution above. We can easily avoid creating functions named `pulp2()` or `pulp3()` in `pulp_smash.*`.

Hey Syrus,

Thank you for getting back with such a detailed answer. Yes, my PR is certainly not perfect, it was partially also me just getting to know that area of the code and if I would make another PR I would probably do it differently. 

And yes, I think it is very good that you are asking these questions and before anything is rewritten we should answer them.

 - Having some connection resolution on the type could make sense. Was there anything you had in mind already?
- There more I think about it, the more I am against the 'automatic' connection type creation. It is really cool to start off with, but for a slightly bigger sized team, where you might have other people come on/off it has been less maintainable in my experience. You have to explain people that this does not return the type itself, but something wrapped around it etc. And imo while it does make code writing easier it makes its maintenance more difficult (because of the magic one has to be aware of). I think just making it easy to create and return connection types would be the best of both worlds: You have to write a bit more but there is no magic going on and one can understand everything just from reading the app code.
- If we don't have the 'automatic' creation anymore it should be easier to have different connections for the same type: The user is always explicitly creating a connection so if subclassing from `Connection` then creating giving it whatever name they want. If we would have something like `DefaultConnection.for_type(Type)` then you could only create that once per schema.

**tldr;** There's no common best practise about whether to use `tox.ini` or `setup.cfg`, so let's use `tox.ini`.

---

I need to point out that if Tox configuration is in `setup.cfg`, it should be under `[tox:tox]`, not `[tox]` (as it is now). I'm not sure why it seems to work in the latter case sometimes. I have reported the fact that `[tox]` in `setup.cfg` without a prefix works sometimes here: https://github.com/tox-dev/tox/issues/545

I'm not sure there is a best practise in the Python world of whether to use `setup.cfg` or `tox.ini` for Tox configuration. The Tox project seemed to encourage using only `tox.ini` in the past, with `setup.cfg` being an undocumented feature. However, there seems to be a movement among some Python developers to start moving more and more configuration to `setup.cfg` instead of increasing the number of files. For instance, Guido Von Rossum was persuaded to add support for Tox configuration in the `setup.cfg` file in this pull request for the mypy project: https://github.com/python/mypy/pull/2761#issuecomment-279305222

Let's move the configuration to `tox.ini` anyway, since this what our developers (@camilonova, @WhyNotHugo) seem to prefer.

the trouble is that ``bit_reference<> `` is not standardized, so every compiler / C++ library is likely to have its own implementation of this class (possibly even with a different name). Somebody has tried to  submit a draft to the standardization committee, but no luck so far: https://groups.google.com/a/isocpp.org/forum/#!topic/std-proposals/jWR04WWndis

You could try to create bindings with something like
```
typedef decytype(std::declval<std::vector<bool>>()[0]) bit_reference.
py::class_<bit_reference>
   .def(...)
```
though I am not 100% sure what to put there.

Errors or throws a casting exception?

The `class_` constructor executes certain pieces of code to add the class to the Python module and register the conversion, so you need to have this `class_` constructed before you can use the conversion. So the exact way of solving this depends on your further architecture:
- If you want to have a separate Python module that's imported by different other modules that need to use the `Acknowledgment` type, then indeed, you probably want to use `PYBIND11_MODULE`. But, since this creates a Python extension module, this needs to be compiled separately to a `.pyd`/`.so` shared library (in a separate compilation unit, etc.). After that, you should be able to use `py::import` to import this Python library and use the types.
- But what you're currently showing, you just want to have that type conversion, so just put `pybind11::module m("xxx"); pybind11::class_<Acknowledgment>(m, "Acknowledgment").def(pybind11::init<>());` at the start of your `main` function. (But I don't know the overall structure of your project, why you actually need to pass through Python, what other parts of the project need to access these type conversions, etc, etc)

Ok, I understand your issue here now (and I can reproduce it).

I fear the problem is really a design decision of pybind11: the default STL bindings make copies whenever creating a return value or parameter, so it's fundamentally incompatible with the idea of passing around unique pointers.

There is one alternative, which is to mark ``std::list<std::unique_ptr<int>>`` as *opaque* and create your own bindings for that. You can read all about it in the docs.

Notes about flatbuffers:

####  Does the format support native upgrades?

Yes. Either the fields have a version number, like protobuf, or the rely on the order of field declaration.

#### Does it support the 256 bits types?

No, a specific struct for this can be defined and reused.

#### Can do without breaking compatibility?

Adding fields | Renaming fields | Removing Fields | Change types
--- | --- | --- | ---
Yes, at the end | Yes, not in the serialized format | Nope, fields can't be removed | Nope, types can't be changed.

Parallelization of forward-style workflow depends on how many root left in the DAG, because multiple steps could be started as soon as their dependencies have been met/executed. This is the same as makefile style. The major difference is that in make style it is easy to think of branches grown from root (final target) that can be executed in parallel, and in forward-style you have to manually specify `branches` (namely `input: None`) to let them execute in parallel.

Interesting, your code works for me (I tried with both a Theano and TensorFlow backend), and I haven't quite been able to reproduce the error.

Questions:
1. What versions of Keras and TensorFlow are you using?
2. Does it deterministically fail when you create the third actor? Or does the timing matter? E.g., what if you do it in an IPython interpreter and wait a while between creating the actors. In the past I've seen Keras read/write files when it starts up and when multiple processes do this they can conflict. This could be worked around by sleeping for a random duration in the actor constructor.
3. Are you using GPUs? Is it possible that you'r running out of GPU resources (or some other resources) because each session is reserving a bunch of memory (or some resource) for itself?


Other notes:
1. By default Keras uses a Theano backend. I think you're using a TensorFlow backend so I did `export KERAS_BACKEND=tensorflow` before starting Python.
2. In the latest version of Keras (`2.0.2` I believe), `layer_from_config` is no longer defined. I had to downgrad to Keras `1.2.2` to run your example.

@zhijunfu This mechanism is already in place (though not all of the cleanup is implemented).

When a driver dies (either gracefully or ungracefully), it triggers a `DisconnectClient` message handler in the node manager, which runs this code.

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/90ae8f11df178f56f39980e7a97eb96c5e4c2124/src/ray/raylet/node_manager.cc#L581-L590

This event is received by all of the other node managers and processed in 

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/90ae8f11df178f56f39980e7a97eb96c5e4c2124/src/ray/raylet/node_manager.cc#L214-L224

As you can see, a bunch of cleanup still needs to be implemented, but this code path does what you described.

Nothing additional would need to be done to handle job cancellation. If there is a "job manager", it can just send a kill signal to the job process and that should trigger the above cleanup.

    mod.init_optimizer(optimizer='sgd', optimizer_params=(('learning_rate', 0.1), ))
    metric = mx.metric.create('acc')
    batch_end_callback_list = [mx.callback.Speedometer(batch_size=batch_size, frequent=10)]
    # logger.info("model build is finished")

    arg_params, aux_params = mod.get_params()
    arg_keys = arg_params.keys()
    aux_keys = aux_params.keys()
    all_keys = list(arg_keys) + list(aux_keys)
    all_values = list(arg_params.values()) + list(aux_params.values())
    # pdb.set_trace()
    ps = ParameterServer.remote(all_keys, all_values)

    worker_tasks = [worker_task.remote(ps, train_iter, args.binary_model) for _ in range(args.num_workers)]

    for epoch in range(args.epochs):
        train_iter.reset()
        metric.reset()

        for (i, batch) in enumerate(train_iter):
            # pull current weights from ParameterServer and set parameters
            current_weights = ray.get(ps.pull.remote(all_keys))
            current_key_weights = dict(zip(all_keys, current_weights))
            new_arg_params = {key: current_key_weights[key] for key in arg_keys}
            new_aux_params = {key: current_key_weights[key] for key in aux_keys}
            #pdb.set_trace()
            mod.set_params(new_arg_params, new_aux_params)
            # forward, backward, update
            mod.forward(batch, is_train=True)
            mod.update_metric(metric, batch.label)
            mod.backward()
            mod.update()
            batch_end_params = BatchEndParam(epoch=epoch, nbatch=i,
                                             eval_metric=metric,
                                             locals=locals())

            for callback in batch_end_callback_list:
                callback(batch_end_params)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='test lenet on mnist with mxnet')
    parser.add_argument('--epochs', dest='epochs', type=int, default=1, help='set the epoch number')
    parser.add_argument("--num-workers", default=1, type=int,
                        help="The number of workers to use.")
    parser.add_argument("--redis-address", default=None, type=str,
                        help="The Redis address of the cluster.")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    main(args)

Hi @reyang, the CI failures are actually valid because of how Django works.

In `request.META`, all incoming headers are prefixed with `HTTP_`, so `X-Cloud-Trace-Context` gets stored as `HTTP_X_CLOUD_TRACE_CONTEXT` instead of `X_CLOUD_TRACE_CONTEXT`.

I think the best solution here is to wrap `request.META` with an object where `__getitem__` rewrites the key `X-Foo` to `HTTP_X_FOO`, then pass that object into `from_headers`. What do you think?
